hints: Let V(w,b) be the optimal expected gain with w white, b black left; you can always stop (value 0).
solution: Let $V(w,b)$ be the optimal expected gain with $w$ white and $b$ black left, with the option to stop ($0$): $V(w,b)=\max\!\big(0,\tfrac{w}{w+b}(1+V(w-1,b))+\tfrac{b}{w+b}(-1+V(w,b-1))\big)$. Computing up from $V(1,1)=\tfrac12$, $V(2,1)=\tfrac43$, $V(1,2)=0$, $V(2,2)=\tfrac23$, $V(1,3)=0$, we get $V(2,3)=\tfrac25(1)+\tfrac35(-1+\tfrac23)=\tfrac25-\tfrac15=\tfrac15.$

hints: Compare via logs: e^π vs π^e ⟺ π vs e·ln π.
The function ln(x)/x is decreasing for x>e, so ln(e)/e > ln(π)/π.
That gives π > e·ln π, so e^π > π^e ⇒ answer 2.
solution: Taking logs, $e^\pi\gtrless\pi^e\iff \pi\gtrless e\ln\pi$. Since $g(x)=\tfrac{\ln x}{x}$ is decreasing for $x>e$, $\tfrac{\ln e}{e}>\tfrac{\ln\pi}{\pi}$, i.e. $\pi>e\ln\pi$. Hence $e^\pi>\pi^e$: answer $2.$

hints: Use Σ_{n=1}^{N} F_n = F_{N+2}−1 for the numerator.
And Σ_{k=1}^{m} F_{3k} = (F_{3m+2}−1)/2 for the denominator.
Both equal (F_302−1) up to the factor 2 ⇒ ratio = 2.
solution: The numerator is $\sum_{n=1}^{300}F_n=F_{302}-1$. For the every-third sum, $\sum_{k=1}^{100}F_{3k}=\tfrac{F_{302}-1}{2}$. Their ratio is $\dfrac{F_{302}-1}{(F_{302}-1)/2}=2.$

hints: Divide 700000 by 77 and round up.
Let Y=1/X with dY=Y(2dt−dB); apply Itô to X=1/Y.
dX=−Y^{−2}dY + Y^{−3}d[Y], with d[Y]=Y²dt.
Simplifies to dX=X(−dt+dB) ⇒ a=−1,b=1,ab=−1.
solution: Let $Y=1/X$, so $dY=Y(2dt-dB)$ and $d[Y,Y]=Y^2dt$. By Itô on $X=1/Y$: $dX=-Y^{-2}dY+Y^{-3}d[Y,Y]=-\tfrac1{Y^2}\,Y(2dt-dB)+\tfrac1{Y}dt=\tfrac1Y(-2dt+dB+dt)=X(-dt+dB).$ So $a=-1,b=1$ and $ab=-1.$

hints: Each weighing has 3 outcomes (left heavy / right heavy / balance), so k weighings distinguish 3^k cases.
12 beans × 2 (heavy or light) = 24 possibilities; need 3^k ≥ 24.
3²=9<24, 3³=27≥24, and a known 3-weighing scheme works ⇒ 3.
solution: Each weighing yields one of $3$ outcomes, so $k$ weighings distinguish at most $3^k$ cases. There are $12\times2=24$ possibilities (which bean, heavier or lighter), and $3^2=9<24\le27=3^3$. A standard adaptive scheme achieves it in $3$, so the minimum is $3.$

hints: Find the expected number of orange in Y after the transfer.
Expected orange moved from X = 300·(400/500)=240; Y starts with 100 orange.
solution: Moving $300$ of X's $500$ balls brings an expected $300\cdot\tfrac{400}{500}=240$ orange. Urn Y then has $100+240=340$ expected orange out of $800$, so $P(\text{orange})=\tfrac{340}{800}=\tfrac{17}{40}.$

hints: Subtract 200: weights become 1–6. The 206-side has 6 plus two others.
6-side heavier ⟺ 6+a+b > 15−(a+b) ⟺ a+b ≥ 5 (a,b from {1..5}).
solution: Reduce to weights $1$–$6$. The side with $6$ holds $6+a+b$ with $a,b$ from $\{1,\dots,5\}$, the other side $15-(a+b)$. It's heavier iff $6+a+b>15-(a+b)$, i.e. $a+b\ge5$. Only the pairs $\{1,2\}$ and $\{1,3\}$ fail, so the probability is $\tfrac{8}{10}=\tfrac45.$

hints: He had n² planted +146 spare; enlarging to (n+1)² needed 31 more trees.
Final orchard = 89² = 7921.
solution: With $n^2$ trees planted and $146$ spare, enlarging to $(n+1)^2$ required $31$ extra trees: $(n+1)^2=n^2+146+31$, so $2n+1=177\Rightarrow n=88$. The finished orchard has $(n+1)^2=89^2=7921$ trees.

hints: A reroll is worth 3.5, so keep any roll above 3.5 (i.e. 4,5,6).
solution: The reroll has expected value $3.5$, so keep the first roll iff it's $4,5,6$ and otherwise reroll. Then $E=\tfrac16\big(3\cdot3.5+4+5+6\big)=\tfrac{25.5}{6}=4.25.$

hints: Expected flips to get two consecutive heads with P(H)=x is (1+x)/x².
The dealer makes the player lose by setting this expected time to d+1=30.
solution: The expected number of flips to obtain two consecutive heads with head-probability $x$ is $\dfrac{1+x}{x^2}$. To make the player's net expected profit non-positive at $d=29$, the dealer sets this expected time to $30$: $\dfrac{1+x}{x^2}=30\Rightarrow30x^2-x-1=0\Rightarrow x=\dfrac{1+11}{60}=\dfrac15.$ The maximum such probability is $\tfrac15.$

hints: Write eating rates: cow+goat=1/45, cow+goose=1/60, goat+goose=1/90.
c+g+go=1/40 ⇒ together they finish in 40 days.
solution: Let rates (field per day) satisfy $c+g=\tfrac1{45}$, $c+\text{go}=\tfrac1{60}$, $g+\text{go}=\tfrac1{90}$. Adding, $2(c+g+\text{go})=\tfrac1{45}+\tfrac1{60}+\tfrac1{90}=\tfrac1{20}$, so $c+g+\text{go}=\tfrac1{40}$. All three together finish in $40$ days.
